coredata.dc:title: Bifunctional manganese-doped silicon quantum dot-responsive smartphone-integrated paper sensor for visual multicolor/multifluorescence dual-mode detection of nitrite
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Mengzhu
Liu, Bangxiang
Liu, Jinjin
Zhu, Hengjia
Bai, Qinqin
Hu, Panwang
Pan, Jianming
Liang, Hao
Niu, Xiangheng
coredata.dc:description: Considering great threats of nitrite in food toward public health, it is significant to develop portable, dependable, and cost-effective methods to detect the target. In comparison with traditional colorimetric or fluorometric strategies relying on the intensity variation at a single wavelength, multimodal measurement with notable color changes is a more attractive paradigm because of its stronger resistance against external conditions, expanded application scenario, improved reliability, and naked-eye distinguishable readouts. However, it remains a challenge on how to attain this goal. Here we proposed a smartphone-integrated paper sensor based on manganese-doped silicon quantum dots (Mn-SiQDs) for the visual dual-mode multicolor/multifluorescence determination of nitrite. The Mn-SiQDs featured both photoluminescence and oxidase-mimetic catalytic activity, and could stimulate the colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) oxidation to a blue species TMB+. When nitrite existed, the generated TMB+ selectively reacted with the analyte to produce yellow diazotized TMB+. Meanwhile, the photoluminescence of Mn-SiQDs could be suppressed by the diazotized TMB+ via inner filter effect. As a result, with a facile electrostatic assembly of Mn-SiQDs and [Ru(bpy)3]2+, the formed complex Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+ led to the variations of both color and photoluminescence upon different levels of nitrite. By combining the principle with paper strips and smartphone sensing, the fabricated sensor offered naked-eye visible multicolor/multifluorescence dual-mode response toward nitrite, exhibiting excellent specificity, performance stability, and practicability in food analysis. Our work provides an effective tool to achieve the multicolor/multifluorescence bimodal measurement of nitrite, and it will inspire future effort on designing versatile materials for advanced sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrite
Multicolor detection
Multifluorescence sensing
Bimodal analysis
Mn-doped silicon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Oxidase-mimetic activity and luminescent property of Mn-SiQDs
Nitrite affects the Mn-SiQDs catalyzed TMB chromogenic reaction
Assembly of Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+ for fluorescence detection of nitrite

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21605061 ), Start-Up Research Fund of University of South China ( 221RGC011 ), Fujian Key Laboratory of Functional Marine Sensing Materials of Minjiang University ( MJUKF-FMSM2022010 ), Postgraduate Research and Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province ( KYCX22_3604 ), Faculty of Agricultural Equipment of Jiangsu University ( NZXB20210207 ), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province ( 2023JJ40534 ), and Health Commission of Hunan Province .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthetic process of Mn-SiQDs. (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of Mn-SiQDs. (C) TEM image of Mn-SiQDs. (D) Size distribution of Mn-SiQDs. (E) XRD pattern of Mn-SiQDs. (F) and (G) Si 2p and Mn 2p XPS spectra of Mn-SiQDs, respectively.

(A) UV–vis profiles of various systems. (B) Oxidase-like activity comparison of Mn-SiQDs and SiQDs. (C)-(E) EPR spectra to capture superoxide radicals, singlet oxygen, and hydroxyl radicals, respectively. (F) Oxidase-mimicking mechanism illustration of Mn-SiQDs catalyzing the chromogenic substrate TMB. (G) Fluorescence spectra of Mn-SiQDs. (H) Effect of buffer pH on the fluorescence of Mn-SiQDs. (I) Fluorescence stability of Mn-SiQDs (continuous irradiation with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm).

(A) Impact of nitrite on the UV–vis pattern of the Mn-SiQDs+TMB group. (B) CIE chromaticity coordinates calculated according to the UV–vis absorption spectra of the Mn-SiQDs+TMB group containing different concentrations of nitrite. (C) Diazotization between nitrite and TMB+.

(A) Preparation of Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+. (B) Zeta potentials of different materials. (C) FTIR spectra of Mn-SiQDs, [Ru(bpy)3]2+ and Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+. (D) Emission spectra of different materials. (E) Impact of nitrite on the luminescence signal of the Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2++TMB system. (F) Calculated CIE coordinates of the Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2++TMB system containing different levels of NO2 -.

(A) Construction process of the portable NO2 - detection platform equipped with smartphone sensing and paper strips. (B) Linear relationship between the multicolor G/B value and the level of nitrite. (C) Linear relationship between the multifluorescence G/B value and the level of nitrite. (D) and (E) Multicolor and multifluorescence responses of different species to verify the selectivity of the paper sensor, respectively. (F) and (G) Multicolor and multifluorescence responses to confirm the storage stability of the portable sensor, respectively.

(A) Schematic of hollow MnFeO for nitrite colorimetric sensing reported in our previous work [24]. (B) Illustration of designing Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+ for advanced nitrite sensing in this work. (C) Schematic illustration of naked-eye visible multicolor/multifluorescence bimodal detection of nitrite with a smartphone-integrated paper sensor based on Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+.

head.ce:title: Bifunctional manganese-doped silicon quantum dot-responsive smartphone-integrated paper sensor for visual multicolor/multifluorescence dual-mode detection of nitrite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, P.R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Public Health, Hengyang Medical School, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Public Health, Hengyang Medical School, University of South China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Public Health, Hengyang Medical School, University of South China, Hengyang 421001, P.R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangsu University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Considering great threats of nitrite in food toward public health, it is significant to develop portable, dependable, and cost-effective methods to detect the target. In comparison with traditional colorimetric or fluorometric strategies relying on the intensity variation at a single wavelength, multimodal measurement with notable color changes is a more attractive paradigm because of its stronger resistance against external conditions, expanded application scenario, improved reliability, and naked-eye distinguishable readouts. However, it remains a challenge on how to attain this goal. Here we proposed a smartphone-integrated paper sensor based on manganese-doped silicon quantum dots (Mn-SiQDs) for the visual dual-mode multicolor/multifluorescence determination of nitrite. The Mn-SiQDs featured both photoluminescence and oxidase-mimetic catalytic activity, and could stimulate the colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) oxidation to a blue species TMB+. When nitrite existed, the generated TMB+ selectively reacted with the analyte to produce yellow diazotized TMB+. Meanwhile, the photoluminescence of Mn-SiQDs could be suppressed by the diazotized TMB+ via inner filter effect. As a result, with a facile electrostatic assembly of Mn-SiQDs and [Ru(bpy)3]2+, the formed complex Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+ led to the variations of both color and photoluminescence upon different levels of nitrite. By combining the principle with paper strips and smartphone sensing, the fabricated sensor offered naked-eye visible multicolor/multifluorescence dual-mode response toward nitrite, exhibiting excellent specificity, performance stability, and practicability in food analysis. Our work provides an effective tool to achieve the multicolor/multifluorescence bimodal measurement of nitrite, and it will inspire future effort on designing versatile materials for advanced sensing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a common nitrogen-containing species in nature, nitrite (NO2 -) is broadly utilized as a preservative and coloring agent in food industry [1]. Whereas, excessive ingestion of nitrite can result in acute poisoning, and long-term consumption even causes esophageal cancer, gastric cancer, and some other diseases, posing huge threats to human health [2]. Hence, it is very essential to develop a suitable sensing platform to conveniently monitor nitrite in food matrices. Up to now, several approaches for nitrite quantification have been established, such as chromatography [3,4], capillary electrophoresis [5], and electrochemical and optical methods [6–12]. Among these approaches, fluorometry and colorimetry are identified as the most potential portable methods for nitrite determination because of their advantages of rapid response and simple operation. Nevertheless, the optical sensors developed currently provide results only by a single-signal colorimetric or fluorescence output, making it difficult to eliminate the interference from external conditions. It is necessary to develop novel paradigms with enhanced reliability and widened application scenario for nitrite analysis.

In recent years, nanozymes, as a product of revolutionary changes brought by the rapid development of nanoscience, appear in scientific research [13–15]. Compared with biological enzymes, these materials with enzyme-like catalytic characteristics exhibit the merits of low cost, facile preparation, superior stability, and adjustable activity. Importantly, the catalytic signal amplification feature confers high sensitivity to biochemical analysis [16–19]. More importantly, apart from enzyme-like catalytic characteristics, many nanozymes can exhibit some other properties, such as optical or magnetic characteristics [20–23]. Through uniting enzyme-mimicking activity with these additional properties in one material, the nanozymes with multiple functions pave a new road to nitrite detection. In this regard, we previously developed a hollow MnFeO material simulating the activity of oxidase [24]. By combining the nanozyme with the diazotization interaction between nitrite and catalysate, we realized the efficient detection of nitrite by ratiometric colorimetry ( Scheme 1A). However, some potential drawbacks exist in the developed sensing system: (ⅰ) the preparation of hollow MnFeO needs multiple steps with a long period and relatively complex processes; (ⅱ) the detection mode output of the sensing system is single, which is difficult to be applied to different scenarios; (ⅲ) the detection system cannot meet the requirement of in-field quick inspection. Hence, it is still urgent to explore multifunctional nitrite detection platforms that are simple, easy to operate and quantified on site.

At present, multi-mode sensing strategies are becoming increasingly popular [25–27]. On the one hand, to meet the need of determining targets in complicated environments, multimodal methods can provide optional suitable modes to realize target monitoring, while traditional single-mode sensors do not have this capacity [28]. In addition, the readouts gained by diverse modes can be calibrated with each other, thus effectually enhancing the reliability of target detection via avoiding false outputs [29]. Therefore, it is highly desired to reasonably design sensing approaches to acquire the multimodal perception of targets.

With the above considerations, here we developed a smartphone-integrated paper sensor utilizing bifunctional manganese-doped silicon quantum dots (Mn-SiQDs) to realize the naked-eye visible multicolor/multifluorescence dual-mode quantification of nitrite in food (Scheme 1B). Different from the previous assay (Scheme 1A), the Mn-SiQDs feature both stable luminescence and excellent oxidase-mimetic catalytic activity, and can trigger the colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) oxidation to a blue intermedium TMB+. If the target nitrite is added, the TMB+ intermedium can specifically interact with the analyte to generate yellow diazotized TMB+ (Scheme 1C). At the same time, the photoluminescence of Mn-SiQDs is suppressed by the diazotized TMB+ through inner filter effect (IFE). When [Ru(bpy)3]2+, a common fluorescein with good chemical stability and versatility [30], is further employed as a fluorescent reference, the Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+ complex formed by a facile electrostatic assembly results in the variations of both color and photoluminescence upon different amounts of the target. Thus, such a bimodal strategy equipped with paper strips and smartphone sensing enables the visual multicolor/multifluorescence sensing of nitrite, providing excellent specificity, performance stability, result reliability and practicability in food safety analysis.
All experimental details were provided in Supplementary Information (SI).

In the present study, the Mn-SiQDs were synthesized with a straightforward one-step hydrothermal strategy using N-[3-(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine (DAMO), sodium citrate dihydrate (SC) and MnCl2·4H2O as precursors ( Fig. 1 A). DAMO serves as the silicon source, and SC is a common reducing agent facilitating the rapid hydrolysis and reduction of silanes to generate silicon cores. The synthetic process is very simple, and all the precursors are readily available. The ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectrum shows a broad absorption from 200 nm to 450 nm (Fig. 1B). Since the Mn-SiQDs with a brown color are composed of SiQD cores and surface groups, the UV–vis profile presents three notable shoulder peaks, among which the weak peak at 250 nm is assigned to the π-π * transition of CC bond, the one at ∼360 nm is caused by the n-π * transition of CO/CN on the surface of Mn-SiQDs, and the shoulder signal around 285 nm originates from the Γ-Γ direct band gap transition of SiQD cores [31,32]. Therefore, the UV–vis result suggests the successful preparation of Mn-SiQDs via the hydrothermal process. The TEM image (Fig. 1 C) indicates an excellent monodispersity of the collected Mn-SiQDs in water, with a lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm. The particle size is relatively uniform, with a mean diameter of 4.3 nm (Fig. 1D). The XRD measurement (Fig. 1E) offers a broad diffraction peak at approximately 2θ = 22°, confirming that the Mn-SiQDs are in an amorphous phase [33].

To analyze the surface chemistry of Mn-SiQDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed. As shown in Fig. S1 (SI), the survey XPS of Mn-SiQDs demonstrates the presence of C, N, O and Si, and the existence of Mn 2p confirms the successful doping of Mn species into SiQDs. The content of Mn atoms in the Mn-SiQDs is determined to be 0.94 % by XPS. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS (Fig. S2A, SI) indicates the presence of CSi, CC/CC, CN, CO and CN/CO at 284.3, 284.9, 285.0, 286.2 and 287.9 eV, respectively [33,34]. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. S2B, SI) is composed of three peaks centered at 398.6 eV (N–Si), 399.8 eV (N–C) and 400.9 eV (N–H) [34]. In the O 1 s profile (Fig. S2C, SI), two peaks appearing at 530.8 and 531.8 eV should be assigned to O–Si bond, and the peak at 532.5 eV is signified to O–C bond [35]. The Si 2p peaks at 101.0, 101.6 and 102.3 eV (Fig. 1 F) can be ascribed to Si−C, Si−N and Si−O bonds, respectively. We synthesized the Mn-SiQDs using DAMO as a silicon source, so the material contains C−O bond, and the Si−N bond may originate from the hydrothermal synthesis of Mn-SiQDs [34]. As depicted in Fig. 1 G, the high-resolution Mn 2p XPS exhibits signals centered at 652.5 and 640.6 eV, assigned to the Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2 species, respectively. The shake-up signals demonstrate the existence of trivalent/divalent Mn states [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidase-mimetic activity and luminescent property of Mn-SiQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the oxidase-like characteristic of Mn-SiQDs, common TMB was utilized as a substrate for color reactions. As presented in Fig. 2 A, when Mn-SiQDs is incubated with the substrate in an acidic buffer (pH 3.5) containing dissolved oxygen, the colorless TMB substrate is rapidly oxidized to a blue intermedium TMB+, and a significant UV–vis signal located at 652 nm is observed. In contrast, the absorption signal is significantly reduced when the reaction is carried out in the buffer with N2 saturation, suggesting that dissolved oxygen plays a critical role in the above process. This result can demonstrate an oxidase-like activity of the Mn-SiQDs catalyzing the TMB oxidation reaction [37]. To understand the oxidase-mimicking activity origin of Mn-SiQDs, SiQDs without Mn doping were prepared as a control counterpart. No capacity to trigger the TMB chromogenic reaction under identical environments is observed in SiQDs (Fig. 2B), implying that the oxidase-mimicking catalytic characteristic of Mn-SiQDs roots in the doped Mn species with Mn3+/Mn2+ mixed states [38].

To verify the catalytic mechanism of Mn-SiQDs, the generation of possible intermediates was explored through electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) measurements. As depicted in Fig. 2 C, in the Mn-SiQDs suspension peak signals attributed to the formation of superoxide free radicals are captured by the catching agent 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO). Fig. 2D displays the characteristic peaks of singlet oxygen, indicating the generation of 1O2 in the Mn-SiQDs suspension. Besides, typical signals ascribed to hydroxyl radicals are also captured in the system (Fig. 2E). These results suggest that the Mn-SiQDs, as an artificial oxidase, first activate dissolved O2 to produce abundant O2 ·−, 1O2, and·OH (Fig. 2 F), which then induces the colorless TMB oxidation to a blue intermedium TMB+ [39]. In detail, the Mn3+/Mn2+ active sites on Mn-SiQDs can catalyze dissolved oxygen into O2 ·− (Fig. S3A, SI), which is subsequently converted to 1O2 and·OH through a disproportionation reaction under acidic conditions [40]. The formed·OH can stimulate the conversion of colorless TMB to blue TMB+ (Fig. S3B, SI) through a single electron process [41,42]. To maximumly obtain the oxidase-like catalytic activity of Mn-SiQDs, the impact of buffer pH on the catalyzed color reaction was studied. As a result, the catalytic feature of Mn-SiQDs highly relies on the pH condition (Fig. S4, SI), exhibiting the best catalytic performance at pH 3.5. In contrast to protein-based natural enzymes, the Mn-SiQDs serving as an inorganic material exhibit a superior stability for storage at regular conditions. Over 60 % catalytic activity is still retained after a storage period of 60 days (Fig. S5, SI).

Next, the luminescent property of Mn-SiQDs was evaluated by fluorescence measurements. As depicted in Fig. 2 G, the Mn-SiQDs show an excitation peak at approximately 360 nm, and the maximum emission wavelength is located at 460 nm. The low-concentration Mn-SiQDs suspension (1 mg/mL) is nearly colorless under visible light, while a quite bright blue luminescence is found under an ultraviolet lamp (365 nm, 2 W). With the increase of excitation wavelength from 330 nm to 400 nm, the emission peak located at 460 nm does not shift, revealing an excitation wavelength-independent emission characteristic of the Mn-SiQDs (Fig. S6A, SI). Such a characteristic is caused by the fact that the surface defects of Mn-SiQDs are effectively passivated by functional groups such as C−N, C−O and CO, and only a single transition mode of certain energy occurs during the fluorescence process [43]. The maximum emission intensity is obtained when an excitation wavelength of 360 nm is applied (Fig. S6B, SI). The luminescent quantum yield of Mn-SiQDs is measured to be 32.7 %, lower than that of SiQDs (43.0 %) (Table S1, SI), implying that the doping of Mn can weaken the quantum yield of SiQDs [36].

The stability of Mn-SiQDs was evaluated considering buffer pH, illumination time and ionic strength. According to Fig. 2H, the Mn-SiQDs present a pH-reliant luminescent behavior. The luminescence intensity increases with the increase of buffer pH, reaching a maximum value at pH 5.0, and then tends to be stable at pH 5.0–9.0. After a continuous irradiation at 365 nm for 180 min, the luminescence intensity of Mn-SiQDs reduces to some extent (Fig. 2I). In contrast, the influence of NaCl concentration is not obvious (Fig. S7, SI), suggesting a strong fluorescence resistance of the Mn-SiQDs against ionic strength. Summarily, compared to carbon-based quantum dots, silicon-based quantum dots as a new type of semiconductor quantum dots have advantages in terms of optical stability, water solubility and photoluminescence [32]. In addition, the loading capacity of silicon-based quantum dots on paper-based materials is superior to that of carbon-based quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nitrite affects the Mn-SiQDs catalyzed TMB chromogenic reaction
ce:section.ce:para: In the study, the influence of nitrite addition on the TMB color reaction triggered by oxidase-mimicking Mn-SiQDs was investigated. The Mn-SiQDs+TMB group appears a blue color due to the catalytic oxidation of TMB to TMB+. After nitrite is further added, interactions exist between the additive and the Mn-SiQDs+TMB system, weakening the UV–vis signal at 652 nm, but the peak at 445 nm reversely increases ( Fig. 3 A). Correspondingly, the solution color changes from blue to green and further to yellow. The CIE coordinates also demonstrate such a color transformation with the increasing content of nitrite added to the Mn-SiQDs+TMB system (Fig. 3B). Contrastively, the UV–vis spectrum of the Mn-SiQDs+NO2 - system (Fig. S8, SI) presents no signal, revealing that no direct reaction exists between Mn-SiQDs and NO2 -. According to our previous studies [24,29,44], nitrite can diazotize with TMB+ in the acidic environment (pH 3.5). In detail, the aromatic primary amine group in TMB+ is diazotizated with nitrite to generate a diazo group (Fig. 3 C), thus leading to a decrease in the 652 nm signal but an increase in the one at 445 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly of Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+ for fluorescence detection of nitrite
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned earlier, single-emission fluorescence signals are susceptible to background, testing environment, and instrument. With the consideration, we used fluorescent [Ru(bpy)3] 2+ as a reference probe and prepared the Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+ complex via an electrostatic assembly of negatively charged Mn-SiQDs and positively charged [Ru(bpy)3]2+, trying to realize the purpose of visual multifluorescence detection of nitrite ( Fig. 4 A). As presented in Fig. S9 (SI), the UV–vis profile of Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+ is similar to that of [Ru(bpy)3]2+, showing two typical peaks of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ at 285 nm and 452 nm [45]. In contrast, the Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+ complex shows an XRD profile similar to that of Mn-SiQDs, and no obvious signal attributed to [Ru(bpy)3]2+ is observed in the material, suggesting that the amount of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ on Mn-SiQDs is very small or the assembled [Ru(bpy)3]2+ is amorphous (Fig. S10, SI). As demonstrated in Fig. 4B, the Zeta potentials of Mn-SiQDs and [Ru(bpy)3]2+ are − 12.37 mV and + 4.95 mV, respectively, enabling the electrostatic assembly of the two components to form Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) size distribution of Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+ reflects a size increase of Mn-SiQDs when integrating with [Ru(bpy)3]2+ via electrostatic forces (Fig. S11, SI). Besides, an average size distribution at 80.4 nm is observed, which should be attributed to the slight aggregation of Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+.

To identify the surface structure of Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) measurements were performed. The signal at 3070 cm−1 confirms the existence of CH stretching vibration in Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+, and the signal at 780 cm−1 belongs to the bending vibration of N–H bond (Fig. 4 C). The above signals belong to pyridine aromatic heterocyclic ring in [Ru(bpy)3]2+ [46]. The stretching vibration of C−H offers a signal at 2935 cm−1, the absorbance at 1575 cm−1 is assigned to N–H bending vibration, and the peak centered at 1388 cm−1 should be attributed to CN stretching vibration. The signals at 1110, 1045 and 915 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of Si–O bond, and the FTIR peak at 690 cm−1 is assigned to N–H bending vibration [34,47]. These characteristic peaks belong to Mn-SiQDs. All the results again confirm the successful assembly of Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+.

It can be seen from Fig. 4D that the Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+ complex exhibits double emissions at 460 nm and 600 nm under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, which are attributed to the characteristic peaks of Mn-SiQDs and [Ru(bpy)3]2+, respectively. To check the stability of Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+, effect of buffer pH on the fluorescence of the complex was studied. As shown in Fig. S12 (SI), the Mn-SiQDs part presents a pH-reliant luminescent behavior, while [Ru(bpy)3]2+ is hardly impacted by pH. When nitrite is present in the Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2++TMB system, the emission peak at 460 nm is greatly quenched, but the reference peak at 600 nm is unchanged (Fig. 4E). The fluorescence quenching of Mn-SiQDs at 460 nm is ascribed to the formation of diazotized TMB+, which can cause an inner filter effect (IFE) on the luminescence of Mn-SiQDs [44]. Correspondingly, the fluorescence color changes from blue to green. The CIE chrominance coordinates (Fig. 4 F) further confirm such a fluorescence variation. Contrastively, fluorescence quenching does not occur in the Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2++TMB or Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2++NO2 - systems (Fig. S13, SI). Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetimes of Mn-SiQDs before and after TMB and NO2 - addition were studied. As demonstrated in Fig. S14 (SI), the luminescence lifetime of Mn-SiQDs changes little. The result also demonstrates the IFE induced quenching of fluorescent Mn-SiQDs by diazotized TMB+.

The synchronous variations of naked-eye visible color and fluorescence offer an effective pathway to achieve the multicolor/multifluorescence bimodal detection of nitrite. To facilitate the assay process and to realize instrument-free detection of NO2 - on site, a smartphone-integrated portable paper-based detection platform was established. As illustrated in Fig. 5 A, the sensing platform is composed of Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+ modified paper strips, a smartphone with an in-built color analysis and data processing App, and a 3D-printed holder equipped with a daylight lamp (2 W, providing stable light for smartphone photographing) and an ultraviolet lamp (365 nm, 2 W, providing excitation light for fluorescence measurement). When TMB is drop-cast onto a paper strip, the paper loaded with Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+ changes from colorless to blue rapidly. When NO2 - is further added, the paper color changes from blue to green and eventually turns to yellow. Meanwhile, the fluorescence of Mn-SiQDs on the paper is gradually suppressed, while the fluorescence of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ gradually becomes prominent, thus leading to a fluorescence change from blue to yellow. Given no remarkable difference between Mn-SiQDs and Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+ responsive to the TMB catalytic oxidation reaction is observed (Fig. S15, SI), the above results verify the feasibility of using Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+ to realize the multicolor/multifluorescence sensing of nitrite.

To better detect the target, some important factors including the ratio of Mn-SiQDs:[Ru(bpy)3]2+, the concentration of TMB used and diazotization reaction time on paper strips were optimized. In the present study, Whatman cellulose paper (CP) was directly employed as a support, because the Whatman CP has an appropriate thickness and abundant porosity for color display and preservation [48]. As for the ratio of Mn-SiQDs:[Ru(bpy)3]2+ and TMB content, the most significant G/B value change is obtained when the ratio of Mn-SiQDs:[Ru(bpy)3]2+ and TMB concentration are 2:1 and 0.28 mM, respectively (Fig. S16, SI). With respect to diazotization reaction time, the G/B value tends to be stable after a reaction time of 15 min. As a result, with the increase of NO2 - level, the colors of the paper strips give rise to obvious changes from blue to yellow at the multicolor and multifluorescence modes, respectively (Fig. 5B and C). Under optimal conditions, the G/B values converted from color signals show good linear correlations with NO2 - concentration in the scales of 0–500 and 0–300 μM, respectively, and the limit of detection (LOD) in multicolor or multifluorescence sensing are determined to be 2.9 and 18.3 μM, respectively. In comparison with other strategies for NO2 - detection (Table S2, SI), the established visual multicolor/multifluorescence bimodal strategy shows comparable advantages in terms of linear range and sensitivity. Importantly, the performance obtained can well meet the quantitative requirements of the analyte in food samples. More significantly, the multicolor/multifluorescence dual-mode approach enables on-site instrument-free detection of the target. Also, the readouts gained from the two modes can be mutually verified and self-corrected, thus improving the reliability for in-field detection.

To evaluate the specificity of the paper-based sensing platform toward nitrite, a range of common cations (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+ and Cr3+), anions (Cl−, Se2−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO4 2− and PO4 3−) and possibly coexisting substances (urea, glucose, glutamic acid (Glu) and phenylalanine (Phe)) were tested. The results (Figs. 5D and 5E) show that these common substances have an ignorable impact on the determination of nitrite using the multicolor or multifluorescence modes, suggesting a good anti-interference ability of the bimodal sensor for the target. Such a good selectivity is mainly ascribed to the specific diazotization interaction of nitrite and TMB+. The storage stability of the paper sensor was also assessed. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 F and G, the paper strips can still work normally after being stored at room temperature for half a month. When nine paper strips prepared from different batches are used to record the multicolor/multifluorescence responses of NO2 -, no significant difference is observed (Fig. S17 and S18, SI), revealing an excellent reproducibility of the proposed sensor. At the same time, smartphone lenses with different pixels cause no obvious experimental bias in the stable smartphone sensing platform [48]. All the experimental results suggest that the paper sensor have a good storage stability and detection repeatability.

In the end, to inspect the reliability and practicability of the fabricated portable sensor, real foods, including Szechuan pickle, salted egg, and sausage, were tested by the standard addition method. The recovery rates of the naked-eye visible dual-mode multicolor/multifluorescence detection are 86.2–103.0 % and 96.8–102.5 %, respectively (Table S3, SI), demonstrating that our method has a good reliability in food sample analysis. With the attractive performance as well as simple operation and in-field detection, the proposed multimodal approach will find potential use in monitoring nitrite for food safety.

In summary, here we have developed a portable smartphone-integrated paper-based platform to realize the naked-eye visible dual-mode multicolor/multifluorescence detection of nitrite via designing multifunctional Mn-SiQDs/[Ru(bpy)3]2+ as a sensing element. By utilizing TMB as an initial chromogenic reagent, ingenious coupling of oxidase-like catalysis and diazotization enables multicolor measurement of the analyte, and rational integration of quenchable Mn-SiQDs with luminescent [Ru(bpy)3]2+ as an internal reference makes it possible to achieve the multifluorescence sensing of nitrite. Thanks to the attractive features of double-signal outputs and naked-eye distinguishable readouts as well as excellent resistance against external environments, facile quantification of the analyte with qualified sensitivity, selectivity and reliability has been verified, showing great promise in food safety analysis. Our contribution offers not only a versatile SiQDs-based sensing unit but also a visual multicolor/multifluorescence bimodal method for nitrite determination, which will inspire the exploration of multifunctional materials and their advanced biochemical sensing applications.

Conceptualization: Xiangheng Niu, Mengzhu Wang, Jianming Pan, Investigation: Mengzhu Wang, Methodology: Mengzhu Wang, Bangxiang Liu, Jinjin Liu, Hengjia Zhu, Xiangheng Niu, Project administration: Xiangheng Niu, Jianming Pan, Software: Mengzhu Wang, Qinqin Bai, Hao Liang, Panwang Hu, Supervision: Xiangheng Niu, Validation: Bangxiang Liu, Jinjin Liu, Hengjia Zhu, Visualization: Mengzhu Wang, Qinqin Bai, Hao Liang, Xiangheng Niu, Writing - original draft: Mengzhu Wang, Writing - review & editing: Xiangheng Niu, Jianming Pan.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21605061), Start-Up Research Fund of University of South China (221RGC011), Fujian Key Laboratory of Functional Marine Sensing Materials of Minjiang University (MJUKF-FMSM2022010), Postgraduate Research and Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX22_3604), Faculty of Agricultural Equipment of Jiangsu University (NZXB20210207), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2023JJ40534), and Health Commission of Hunan Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.134143.

ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mengzhu Wang is a master candidate with the supervision of Prof. Niu, and she focuses her research interest on nitrite sensing using nanozymes.
Bangxiang Liu is a master candidate with the supervision of Prof. Niu, and he focuses his interest on nanozyme-based analysis.
Jinjin Liu is an assistant engineer in Prof. Niu’s group in University of South China. She is studying on the preparation of new nanozymes and their biochemical sensing applications.
Hengjia Zhu is a doctoral candidate, and he focuses on nanozyme-based biosensors for pesticide residue detection.
Qinqin Bai is a teacher working in Prof. Niu’s group in University of South China, and she is interested in phage-based sensors.
Panwang Hu is a master candidate of Jiangsu University, and he focuses on the fabrication of heavy metal ion sensing systems using advanced nanozymes.
Jianming Pan is a professor working in Jiangsu University, and he is interested in chemical separation and analysis.
Hao Liang is a teacher working in Prof. Niu’s group in University of South China, and she is interested in nanozyme-based aptamer sensors.
Xiangheng Niu is a Professor now working in University of South China, and he is interested in nanozyme-based analytical methods.
